free_text: Neutron Particle Hole Structure in 208Pb Through
Isobaric Analog Resonances

BP
The LASL three-stage Van de Graaff
accelerator.
Elbeck spectrograph.
The target was a self-
supporting 208Pb (99.3% enriched) metallic foil of 0.7
mg/cm2 thickness, mounted in transmission position at
an angle of 45 degrees.
Tentative value.
Table I, page 64 of Thesis Kulleck.
The University of Texas Van de Graaff
accelerators in three-stage operation.
The targets were made by
evaporating lead metal enriched to 99% in 208Pb from
a tungsten boat onto glass slides that were
previously coated with a very thin film of a
detergent. It turned out to be 0.71 mg/cm2. This
corresponds to an energy loss of about 7.5 keV for
16.0-MeV protons as they penetrate through the target.
Four 3-mm-thick ORTEC SiLi detectors were used
in this experiment. They were placed at 90, 12, 150,
and 170 degrees to the incident beam and cooled to
approximately dry-ice temperature.
Fig. 3, page 1314 of Phys.Rev.165(1968)1312.

Table I, page 1317 of Phys.Rev.165(1968)1312.
The three stage FN Van de Graaff at the
University of Washington.
The target used for the
experiment was a 99% enriched 208Pb self-supporting
metallic film. The effective thickness of the 208Pb
was 300 microg/cm2.
The charged-particle spectra were obtained
with three 2-mm Si(Li) detectors which were
positioned at 10 in intervals and cooled to dry-ice
temperatures.
Fig. 2, page 1494 of Phys.Rev.C2(1970)1491

injector.

MeV.
The target used for the
experiment was a 99% enriched 208Pb self-supporting
metallic film. The effective thickness of the 208Pb
was 300 microg/cm2.

with three 2-mm Si(Li) detectors which were
positioned at 10 in intervals and cooled to dry-ice
